segments.s648-g165: برین is the fused preposition+demonstrative 'on/among these' (بر + این in poetic spelling). همنشان means 'those who sit together' / companions here; روز is the object/time 'the day'. بگذاشتند is past 'they spent/they put (the day down)' — in context it means 'they spent the day.' Word order is compact/poetic (location/demonstrative + companions + day + verb).
segments.s648-g166: همی is an adverbial particle (here 'indeed/so/thus') modifying the statement. راه را uses را as the definite object marker; خانه is the predicate nominal 'house/home'. پنداشتند is past 'they thought/considered' — together 'they considered the road to be a house' i.e. they treated the road as home. This is a standard Persian construction where پنداشتن takes a direct object + complement.
segments.s648-g167: چو here means 'when' (a temporal subordinating conjunction). آگاهی 'information/news' is the subject that 'came' (آمد) to سوی 'toward' (sūy) + خوشنواز, an epithet/name meaning 'the melodious one' or a person called Khoshnavāz; the clause means 'when news reached Khoshnavāz'.
segments.s648-g168: به دشت آمد is straightforward 'he came to the plain' (به = to, دشت = plain, آمد = came). و joins the next action. جنگ را کرد ساز is a compact poetic expression: جنگ را (the war/battle as definite object) + کرد (made/caused) + ساز (literally 'instrument/means' but here idiomatically 'preparations/arrangements'); together 'he made preparations for battle' or 'he made ready for war.' The object marker را marks جنگ as the thing for which preparations are made.
segments.s648-g321: همانگه is an adverbial time phrase 'at that moment'. فروهشت is a poetic past form meaning 'spread out' or 'strewn' (verb precedes its object here for brisk, poetic word order). پرده‌سرای is the object 'curtain-hall' / 'curtained hall' (a compound noun); together the phrase describes that the curtained space was spread/arranged at that instant.
segments.s648-g322: سپهبد is the subject 'the spahbed' (a military title). باسب is an adverbial/phrase indicating relation to a horse ('on/by horse'); اندر آورد پای is a light verb construction where آورد (bring) takes پای ('foot') as object — literally 'brought his foot inside' = 'dismounted'. The clause uses simple past narration and an implied actor (the spahbed) performing both actions.
segments.s648-g323: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (here marking the river as the point crossed). جیحون is the river name (Amu Darya). گذر کرد is a compound verb (گذر + کرد) meaning 'made passage/ crossed'; کرد is the past auxiliary. پیروز و شاد are adjectives (postpositive in poetry) describing the subject: 'victorious and joyful.'
segments.s648-g324: This is a nominal apposition: 'ابا نامور موبد' is read as a name plus epithet and title — 'Aba Namvar, the renowned mobed' — followed by 'و کیقباد' ('and Kay Qobad'). Here ابا functions as part of a proper name; it is not the ordinary verb 'to refuse', so the conventional lexical senses do not apply.
segments.s648-g325: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. آگاهی آمد is an intransitive light-verb phrase 'news/information came' (آگاهی 'news/information' + آمد past 'arrived'). به ایران‌زمین is the destination phrase 'to the land of Iran' and links with آمد to mean 'reached Iran.'
segments.s648-g326: ازان is a demonstrative/genitive marker 'of that / from that' linking back to the prior news. نیک‌پی is an epithet meaning 'well-born' (here describing lineage/quality rather than abstract virtue). مهتر is 'chief/elder' as a title. بفرین is a poetic verb meaning 'they cursed' (the form is terse/archaic and the subject is implied — a common narrative device in epic poetry).
segments.s648-g327: همان is a demonstrative 'that/the same' emphasizing identity. جنگ و پیکار are tautological, synonymous nouns 'war and combat' (a common poetic pair). با خوشنواز means 'with Khushnavaz' — خوشنواز here functions as a proper name/epithet (not just the adjective 'melodious'), indicating the person involved in the fighting.
segments.s648-g328: ز رای is a prepositional phrase 'by/from the counsel' (ز marking cause/agent in poetic usage). چنان is the demonstrative adjective 'such'. مرد is the noun 'man', and نیرنگ‌ساز is a compound noun 'deceiver / schemer' describing him. Together the phrase gives the cause: 'by the counsel/plan of such a scheming man.'
segments.s648-g257: بیامد is a past-tense, 3rd-person singular narrative verb 'came' placed before the subject (typical of older/poetic Persian). فرستاده خوشنواز is a noun phrase 'the messenger, (who is) melodious' where خوشنواز is a postpositive adjective modifying فرستاده.
segments.s648-g258: بگفت is an archaic/poetic past form 'he said' (verbal clause). آنچ is a relative/indefinite pronoun 'that which' heading the reported content; بود is the past copula 'was' linking the predicate that follows. آشکارا and راز form a coordinated predicate 'evident and secret' (آشکارا here = 'manifest/evident', راز = 'secret'), so the clause means 'that which was evident and (that which was) secret'.
segments.s648-g259: چنین 'thus' precedes گفت to mark the manner ('thus said'). In this poetic word order گفت (said) comes before the subject لشکر (the army). که introduces the content clause 'that ...' and فرمان تو راست is a nominal sentence: فرمان (command) + تو (your) + راست (is right/true) where راست is the predicate adjective.
segments.s648-g260: بدین is 'with/by this' (in older Persian written as ب + این). آشتی رای is a compound idea: آشتی (reconciliation/truce) qualifying رای (decision/opinion/plan) — 'the decision for reconciliation' — and و پیمان تو is joined by و to add 'and your covenant/treaty'. The final راست predicates the preceding coordinated nouns, i.e. 'your plan/opinion and your covenant are right.' Note the poetic compaction and older orthography.
segments.s648-g261: به ایران is a locative phrase 'in Iran'. نداند is a negative present/past-form of 'to know/consider' used here as 'does not consider / does not regard'. The subject کسی follows the verb (poetic inversion) and in this negative context functions as 'no one'. از تو به is a prepositional frame 'of you as ...' leading into the complement in the next segment.
segments.s648-g262: بما 'for us/to us' marks the perspective. بر here functions like 'over/on' or 'with regard to', and تویی is the emphatic/poetic second-person copula 'thou art' (you are). The following coordinated nouns شاه و سالار و مه are predicates of تویی (‘you are king and commander and moon’), where مه (moon) is an honorific epithet.
segments.s648-g263: چنین گفت 'thus said' opens the reported speech. با + سرکشان is a prepositional phrase 'with the proud/rebellious ones' (سرکشان is a plural/collective form meaning 'the insolent/rebellious'), and سوفزای is the personal name of the speaker; word order places the verb phrase before the prepositional accompaniment and name.
segments.s648-g264: که introduces the subordinate content clause. امروز 'today' sets the time; ما را is a topical/dative 'for us' (ما را = 'for us' / 'we have'); جزین is the fused form جز این 'except this' and نیست رای is the negated existential 'there is no counsel/opinion' — together: 'that today for us there is no plan/opinion except this.'
segments.s648-g249: نباشد is a negative optative/subjunctive meaning 'let there not be' or 'there will not be' (third-person). مرا (here split as م + را in the data) functions as a dative/ethical object 'for me' (literally 'to me'); را is the object/topic marker attached to it. سوی ایران = 'toward Iran' (prepositional phrase). بسیچ means a large muster/many (here 'a great force/muster'). The whole segment says there will be no great muster of mine toward Iran.
segments.s648-g250: تو is the second-person pronoun 'you'. از عهد بهرام is a prepositional phrase 'from (or with respect to) Bahram's oath/pledge' (عهد = oath/pledge). گردن مپیچ is an idiomatic prohibition: گردن 'neck' + مپیچ (negative imperative/subjunctive 'do not turn/evade') = 'do not turn your neck away', i.e. 'do not evade/break (the pledge)'. The verb form مپیچ is the negative imperative/subjunctive addressing 'تو'.
segments.s648-g251: شهنشاه گیتی = 'the sovereign/emperor of the world' (epithet). ببخشید here is a past third-person verb meaning 'bestowed/gave' (classical usage), and راست functions as an emphatic/adverbial 'truly/indeed'. The clause states that the emperor has indeed granted or given (the matter in question).
segments.s648-g252: This is an inverted predicative construction: 'مرا ترک و چین است' literally 'for me are Turk and China' = 'Turks and China are mine' (مرا = dative/possessive sense 'to me' / 'I have'). را after م is the object/topic marker in the data split. است is the copula 'is/are'. و ایران تو راست follows: 'and Iran is yours indeed' — ایران (Iran) as subject, تو = 'you' (possessor), راست = 'truly/rightly' as emphasis. Classical Persian often places the possessor-object before است in this way to express possession.
segments.s648-g253: چو is the conjunction 'when'. بشنید is past third-person 'he heard'. پیغام او = 'his message' (possessive with pronoun after the noun, a common classical order). سوفراز is the personal name (the agent) placed after the object phrase; in context it functions as the subject who performs the hearing. The sequence thus yields 'When Sofraz heard his message...'.
segments.s648-g254: بیاورد is a past third-person verb 'he brought'. لشکر is the direct object 'army/host'. به + پرده‌سرای indicates direction/place 'to/into the pavilion or curtained-hall' (به here = 'into/toward'). The clause as a whole: 'he brought the army into the pavilion.'
segments.s648-g255: فرستاده را is the definite object marked with را: 'to/toward the messenger' (classical ordering places the marked object before the verb). گفت here stands for 'said/told' (the data's listed senses are not a verb form, so the precise sense index is unavailable). پیش سپاه = 'in the presence of / before the army' (پیش = 'in front of / before' + سپاه 'the army'). The whole clause means 'He addressed/told the messenger in front of the army.'
segments.s648-g256: بگوی is an imperative/subjunctive form meaning 'say / tell' directed to the messenger (classical variant of the command). آنچ = 'that which' (introducing reported content). بشنیدی is second-person past 'you heard' (reporting what the messenger heard). از رزمخواه = 'from the warrior/fighter' (رزمخواه = the combatant/aspiring fighter). Together: 'Tell (them) that which you heard from the warrior.'
segments.s648-g25: که is a relative conjunction 'who/that' introducing a clause describing a person. او is the pronoun 'he' (subject). گاه ... گه is a paired adverbial construction 'sometimes ... sometimes' (گه = sometimes) that frames two predicative expressions. زهرست is a predicative phrase: زهر 'poison' + archaic copula -ست (old form of است) = 'is poison'. پای‌زهر is a nominal predicate 'a bane/poisonous thing' that completes the second half of the 'sometimes ... sometimes' contrast.
segments.s648-g26: مجویید is the negative imperative/hortative plural (or polite singular) of جستن: 'do not seek'. از introduces the source/ingredient 'from' (the thing one might seek). زهر تریاک is a nominal phrase 'the poison of opium' (زهر = poison, تریاک = opium). بهر at the end marks purpose/interest 'for / on account of' and here means 'for any purpose' or 'for advantage'.
segments.s648-g27: ز (poetic form of از) begins a prepositional phrase indicating motive 'for / from'. گیتی 'the world' is the object of that preposition; تو 'your' qualifies خوشنودی 'approval/pleasure' — together 'for the world's (or worldly) approval of you'. شاه‌جوی is a noun 'one who seeks the king/court' and is the role being addressed (i.e. 'a seeker of the king').
segments.s648-g28: مشو is the negative imperative 'do not become / do not be' (second person). پیش تختش is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'before his throne / in the presence of his throne' (پیش = before/in the presence of; تختش = his throne). مگر is the conditional 'unless'. تازه‌روی is a substantive/adjective used as a predicate 'a newcomer / novice' — the clause thus means 'do not go before his throne unless you are a newcomer.'
segments.s648-g29: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. خشم 'anger' is the subject of the subordinate clause. آورد is the classical verb 'bring about / arise' used here as 'arises / comes' (i.e. 'when anger arises'). شاه is the agent 'the king'. پوزش گزین is an imperative pair: پوزش 'pardon/apology' is the thing to be sought and گزین (imperative of گزیدن) means 'choose / take' — 'choose (to seek) pardon'.
segments.s648-g30: همی is an adverb often used in classical/poetic Persian to indicate continuity or emphasis here rendered 'always / ever'. خوان is used here in a literary/archaic way meaning one who calls or addresses (related to خواندن/خوان) — it functions as the verbal notion 'call/address'. به marks the indirect object 'to / toward'. بیداد 'injustice/oppression' and دادآفرین 'giver/creator of justice' appear as the two targets of that addressing; the phrase can be read literally as 'always call to Injustice and the Giver-of-Justice.'
segments.s648-g31: هرآنگه que/که introduces a concessive/temporal clause 'whenever / however'. گویی here is a verbal/formal expression 'you say / you claim' (in poetry the noun/verb forms blur); the following که introduces the quoted content 'that I became wise'. دانا 'wise/learned' is the predicative adjective, and شدم is the past-tense copula 'I became' — together 'you say that I became learned'.
segments.s648-g32: به هر دانشی is a prepositional phrase 'in/with every knowledge' (به = in/regarding; هر = every; دانشی = knowledge/branch of learning). بر here functions as a prepositional/relational marker introducing the domain or standard ('with respect to / regarding') and توانا شدم 'I became capable/able' is the main verb phrase — together conveying 'I have become capable/superior in every branch of knowledge.'
segments.s648-g9: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'; بنشست is past 3rd singular 'he sat down'. بر گاه is a prepositional phrase 'on the throne/at the court' (preposition + noun). گفت is the reporting verb 'said', and ای + ردان forms a vocative address ('O, ___') addressing those present.
segments.s648-g10: بجویید is a 2nd‑person‑plural (polite/imperative) form 'seek' or 'search for'. رای و دل are two parallel objects 'counsel/opinion and heart(s)'. بخردان functions as a nominal modifier 'of the wise' (the wise ones) that governs both رای and دل.
segments.s648-g11: شما را places 'you' as the beneficiary/topic (the archaic/poetic use of را here marks the person for whom something exists). بزرگیست is an existential/predicative construction ('there is greatness' / 'greatness exists'). نزدیک من is a locative phrase 'near me'.
segments.s648-g12: چو again introduces a temporal clause 'when'. روشن شود is a verbal phrase 'becomes clear' (شود = 'become' / auxiliary-like verb). رای تاریک من is a noun phrase 'my dark counsel' (رای = counsel/opinion; تاریک qualifies it; من is the possessor).
segments.s648-g13: به گیتی is a locative phrase 'in the world' (به + گیتی). هر آنکس که introduces a universal relative clause 'whoever/whomever'. نیکی کند is a present/subjunctive statement 'does good' or 'performs good deeds' (کند = 3rd singular present/subjunctive).
segments.s648-g14: بکوشد is an exhortative/subjunctive 'let him strive' or 'should strive'. که / تا introduce the purpose/result clause 'so that'. رای ما نشکند is a negative jussive/subjunctive meaning 'that our counsel not be broken' (نشکند = negative subjunctive of شکستن 'to break').
segments.s648-g15: هر آنکس کجا باشد is a circumstantial/subjunctive clause 'whoever, wherever he may be' (باشد used in the subjunctive/optative sense). او is an emphatic pronoun 'he', and بدسگال is a predicate adjective 'ill‑fated / unlucky'.
segments.s648-g16: که is a relative 'who/that'. خواهد is a modal/future‑like verb 'wishes/will' here meaning 'desires' or 'wants'. همی is an archaic/poetic adverbial particle (here an intensifier 'indeed/ever'). کار خود را is 'his own work' with را marking the definite object, and همال is a noun 'bearer/carrier' used figuratively: the one who bears or carries (i.e., someone on whom the work is imposed). Together the clause describes one who wants another to carry his work.
segments.s648-g209: This is an elliptical noun list (no governing verb here) joined with the coordinating و. سلیح (weapon) begins the catalogue; کمرها is plural with the plural suffix -ها meaning 'belts/girdles'. The conjunctions و simply link the items in the order given (weapon, belts, horse, wayfarer). رهی is a noun meaning 'wayfarer/traveller' and follows the same nominal series.
segments.s648-g210: A continuation of the list. ستام is another noun in apposition (lexical identification uncertain here, but treated as an item in the list). سنان 'spear' follows, then کلاه modified by the adjective مهی. Note that Persian places adjectives after the noun, so کلاه مهی = 'helmet that is moon‑like / shining'.
segments.s648-g211: همی‌برد is an old/imperfective past form meaning 'kept carrying' or 'was carrying off' (iterative past). هر کس is the subject 'each person', and بر + proper name سوفزای gives the direction 'to Sufza' (بر used here as 'toward/to'). The direct object (the spoils) is omitted because it was mentioned earlier; Persian frequently omits a repeated object.
segments.s648-g212: تلی 'a heap/pile' is the subject; گشته is the verbal/adjectival perfect/participial form of شدن used here as 'had become/turned into'. چون introduces a simile 'like', and کوه البرز جای literally 'a place (جای) like Mount Alborz' — Persian word order places the simile after the verb, so literally 'a heap had become, like Mount Alborz, a place.'
segments.s648-g213: ببخشید is a past 3rd‑plural form of بخشیدن here meaning 'they gave/bestowed' (context of distributing spoils), not 'forgave'. یکسر is an adverb 'entirely/at once' modifying the verb; همه is the direct object 'everything/all', and بر سپاه 'to the army' marks the beneficiary with بر + animate noun.
segments.s648-g214: This clause uses an inverted poetic word order: the negated past verb نَکرد appears earlier, and the rest follows. نَکرد = 'did not do' combines with نگاه 'look/attention' as the verbal noun, so together 'did not look/paid no heed'. اندر آن چیز = 'at/in that matter', and ترکان (the Turks) is the logical subject (the usual prose order would be ترکان اندر آن چیز نگاه نکردند).
segments.s648-g215: به لشکر is a dative phrase 'to the army'; چنین گفت is 'said thus' (گفت is the simple past 3rd‑singular 'he said' and چنین 'thus' introduces the reported speech). کامروز کار is the opening of the reported clause 'today the affair/work', with the time expression کامروز placed before the head noun کار.
segments.s648-g216: This is the predicate of the reported speech. به کام ما = 'to our desire/for our satisfaction' (به + noun phrase meaning 'in accordance with'), then بد از روزگار is an idiomatic comment 'bad from/of the times' (بد adjective + از + روزگار 'fate/times'); together the sense is 'the affair goes as we wish; as for the times, they are unfavorable' (the short clause is compact and somewhat elliptical).
segments.s648-g121: This is a noun phrase with a prepositional complement: فرستاده (the messenger) followed by با نامه سوفزای (with Sofzāy's letter). با introduces the accompanying thing ('with'), and نامه سوفزای is a possessive/attributive sequence meaning 'Sofzāy's letter.' Word order places the noun (فرستاده) before the modifying prepositional phrase.
segments.s648-g122: بیامد is a past tense verb 'came.' چو introduces a simile here meaning 'like.' شیر دلاور is a noun + adjective ('a brave/valiant lion'), and ز جای uses the poetic ablative ز (equivalent to modern از) with جای 'place' to mean 'from its/their place' or 'from its lair.' The clause as a whole is a simile modifying how the messenger arrived.
segments.s648-g123: چو here is a temporal conjunction 'when.' آشفته (disturbed/aghast) precedes آمد (came) to express the manner of arrival. بر followed by a person-name/title (خوشنواز) indicates movement toward or arrival before that person; خوشنواز functions here as a personal epithet/title ('the skilled musician' or a proper name).
segments.s648-g124: بشد is an archaic/periphrastic past-form meaning 'he proceeded / he came to be' (narrative past). پیش تخت means 'in the presence of the throne' (پیش = in front of, in the presence of; تخت = throne). و links clauses. ببردش is a verb with the enclitic object -ش ('him/it'); here it means 'he took/performed it' with نماز (ritual prayer) as the object — i.e. 'he performed/said the prayer for him/there.' The verbal forms are classical/poetic rather than modern colloquial.
segments.s648-g125: بدو is the dative/clitic-like form 'to him / unto him' placed before the verb; داد is the past tense 'gave.' پس here means 'then/afterwards' and precedes the object; نامه سوفزای is the object 'Sofzāy's letter.' The clause has inverted ordering for emphasis (dative + verb + temporal particle + object).
segments.s648-g126: همی‌بود is the imperfective past 'it remained / continued to be.' یک چند is an idiomatic expression meaning 'for a short while / a little while.' پیشش = 'before him / in front of him' (with the clitic -ش). بپای is an older verb meaning 'to remain, to wait (at someone's feet)' or 'to persist'; here it conveys that the letter stayed there briefly.
segments.s648-g127: نویسنده is 'the scribe/writer' acting as subject. نامه را داد uses the object marker را to mark نامه (the letter) as definite and داد is 'gave' in the past. و گفت links the action to reported speech — گفت is the past tense 'said' introducing what follows.
segments.s648-g128: که introduces the content clause ('that/that which'); پنهان here functions adverbially 'in secret/privately.' بگوی is the subjunctive/present-stem used to express an instruction 'say' (imperative-like). آنچ = 'that which/what' and is followed by two adjectives joined by و: نرمست ('soft, gentle') and زفت ('foul, nasty' — a pejorative adjective). The clause thus commands: 'say in private whatever is gentle and whatever is foul.'
segments.s648-g337: بزرگان فرزانه is a noun phrase with the modifier after the head (classical Persian order): بزرگان (the great/leading men) modified by فرزانه (wise). برخاستند is a past-tense, 3rd-person plural verb (برخاست + -ند) meaning 'they rose/stood up'; the verb follows the noun phrase as typical in Persian.
segments.s648-g338: پذیره شدن is a verbal noun (masdar) + شدن describing 'the reception taking place'; را is the definite/direct-object marker putting focus/emphasis on that event; بیاراستند is the past 3rd-person plural (بی-آراست-ند) meaning 'they adorned/prepared' and takes the preceding phrase as its object (object-before-verb is common in classical Persian word order).
segments.s648-g339: بلاش is the subject (a proper name). آن زمان is a temporal phrase 'at that time/then' (آن here = 'then'); تخت زرین is the object (throne described by the adjective زرین = 'golden') and precedes the verb. نهاد is the classical past of نهادن 'to put/place' (3rd-person singular) meaning 'he placed/put down'.
segments.s648-g340: که تا introduces a purposive subordinate clause ('so that / in order that'): که + تا + verb gives purpose. برنشیند is a present-subjunctive (3rd-person sing.) of برنشستن 'to sit upon' meaning 'may sit on'. برو here is an archaic contraction (literally بر او = 'on it') marking location 'upon it' (common in older texts). کیقباد is the person for whom the purpose is stated (Kay Qobad).
segments.s648-g341: چو آمد is a temporal clause ('when he came') with چو = 'when' and آمد = past 'came'. به شهر اندرون marks motion into the inner part of the city ('into the inner city'); note the classical omission of the ezafe linking اندرون to the following name. سوفزای is a proper name attached to the phrase (the inner quarter/place of Sofzai).
segments.s648-g342: بزرگان is the subject 'the great men/elders'. برفتند is the past 3rd-person plural of رفتن 'they went/they rose (departed from their seats)'. یک سر ز جای is an idiomatic sequence: یک سر (literally 'one head') + ز جای ('from place') conveys that each rose from his place or that they rose from their places; the phrase functions adverbially to describe how they moved.
segments.s648-g343: پذیره شدن را is again the verbal-noun phrase 'the holding/happening of the reception' marked by را as a definite object. بیاراست is the past 3rd-person singular 'he prepared/adorned'. شاه follows the verb as the subject (verb–subject order is common in epic/classical Persian), so the whole means 'the king prepared the reception.'
segments.s648-g344: همی‌رفت is an imperfective past form (همی- prefix + رفت) meaning 'he was going' or 'he went' with an ongoing nuance. با introduces the accompanying party ('with'). آنک is a relative/demonstrative ('that which' / 'which'), here introducing the clause describing the army; بودش is the enclitic past 'it was his' / 'he had', and سپاه is 'army' — together: 'he went with that which was his army' = 'he went with the army he had.'
segments.s648-g281: This clause is object + object-marker (فرستاده را) followed by the adjective نغز modifying the noun پاسخ and the verb دهیم. را is the definite-object marker attached to فرستاده. دهیم is first-person plural present-subjunctive/hortative ('let us give' / 'we should give') rather than a simple present tense; the whole clause is a hortatory imperative meaning 'let us give the messenger a fine answer.'
segments.s648-g282: درین is the poetic contraction of در + این ('in this'). آشتی is the noun governed by that preposition. رای فرخ is a noun phrase 'a fortunate counsel/plan' where رای (plan/counsel) is followed by the adjective فرخ (fortunate) — classical Persian often places modifiers after the head. نهیم is the first-person plural subjunctive/hortative of نهادن ('let us place/set'), so the clause means 'let us set/place a fortunate plan in this reconciliation' and is coordinated by sense to the previous hortative clause (implied 'and').
segments.s648-g283: مگر here functions as a conditional 'unless' introducing the clause. باز is an adverb 'again' that precedes the verb بینیم. بینیم is the first-person-plural present-subjunctive/optative ('we may see' / 'let us see') used in conditional/hortatory sense. روی قباد is the object phrase 'the face of Qobad' (classical Persian frequently omits the ezāfe linking particle), so the literal order is 'Unless again we see Qobad's face.'
segments.s648-g284: که introduces the following wish/result clause ('that/so that'). بی او is a prepositional phrase 'without him.' سر پادشاهی is an idiomatic collocation meaning 'the head/top of kingship' (= sovereignty or the throne). مباد is an optative/negative jussive meaning 'may it not be' (expressing a wish that something not occur). Together the clause expresses the wish 'that there may be no kingship without him.'
segments.s648-g285: همان is a demonstrative 'that very / the same' modifying موبد (mōbed, Zoroastrian priest). پاکدل is an epithet placed after the noun (common in Persian epic diction) meaning 'pure‑hearted.' The proper name اردشیر follows in apposition. This whole segment is a nominal phrase introducing the person.
segments.s648-g286: کسی را که begins a relative/appositive clause: کسی را ('a person' marked by definite object marker را) + که ('who/that') introduces the clause. بینید is used here in the classical/poetic sense of 'had seen' / 'had witnessed' (a perfective or relative participial sense in context), and برنا و پیر are coordinated adjectives 'young and old' describing what he had seen. Syntactically the clause qualifies the preceding noun phrase: 'a person who had seen youth and old age.'
segments.s648-g287: فرستاده را is the definite object ('the messenger') marked by را. خواند is the simple past third-person singular of خواندن here meaning 'he called/summoned.' پس is an adverb 'then/after that' and پهلوان ('the champion/hero') is the subject introduced after the adverb, a common narrative ordering in Persian epic style.
segments.s648-g288: سخن گفت is a common compound meaning 'spoke' (سخن 'word/speech' + گفت 'said' — here گفت is past third-person). با او marks the addressee 'to him.' به شیرین زبان is a prepositional/manner phrase 'in a sweet tongue' where به marks the manner and شیرین زبان functions as a fixed adjectival phrase 'sweet‑tongued' (shaping how he spoke).
segments.s648-g169: به پیکند شد is an impersonal/past construction meaning ‘messengers were sent’; به here introduces the action and the verb پیکند (send messengers) with شد marking the past/passive sense. رزمگاهی گزید is a coordinated past action: گزید (3rd‑sing. past of گزیدن) ‘chose’ with رزمگاهی (battlefield) as its object; the two clauses are linked as successive actions.
segments.s648-g170: که introduces a relative clause ‘that/which’. چرخ روان is a compound where چرخ (the wheel/heavens/fate) takes روان as an adjectival qualifier ‘moving/flowing’, so ‘the moving wheel/heavens (i.e. fate)’. روی هامون uses روی in the locative/surface sense ‘on/upon’ followed by هامون ‘the plain’. ندید is simple past negative ‘did not see’ (the object ‘its like’ is implied in Persian poetic usage).
segments.s648-g171: وزین روی is a descriptive phrase: وزین (weighty/dignified) modifies روی ‘face/appearance’ — here roughly ‘at that grave/weighty visage’. پر کینه دل modifies دل (heart) with پر کینه ‘full of hatred’, so the whole phrase reads ‘Sofuzāy’s heart, full of hatred at that appearance’. سوفزای is the proper name in genitive = ‘Sofuzāy’s heart’.
segments.s648-g172: به کردار باد is a simile: به کردار ‘in the manner/guise of’ plus باد ‘the wind’ — ‘as the wind’. اندر آمد ز جای is a standard perfective/past phrase ‘came up/rose from his place’ (اندر = in/from within, آمد = came, ز جای = from his spot). The sequence yields ‘rose from his place like the wind.’
segments.s648-g173: چو ... شد is a temporal subordinate clause ‘when ... became’. شب تیره شد = ‘when the night became dark’. پهلوان سپاه is the subject phrase ‘the champion (of the) army’ (pahlavān qualifying or identified with the army’s commander).
segments.s648-g174: به پیلان here is instrumental/associative ‘with/by (the) elephants’; آسوده is a predicative/adjectival state ‘at ease/resting’ and bربست (بربست) is the past verb ‘shut/closed/blocked’; راه is the object ‘the road/way’. Poetic word order places the descriptive آسوده before the verb: ‘with the elephants at rest he blocked the road.’
segments.s648-g175: طلایه (vanguard) is the subject and همی‌گشت is an iterative/imperfective past ‘kept moving/went about’. بر هر دو سوی is a locative phrase ‘on/both sides’ (بر = on/upon/toward, هر دو سوی = both directions/sides). The clause describes vanguard detachments circulating on both sides.
segments.s648-g176: جهان شد is ‘the world became’ (شد = became/happened). پر + noun is a common classical construction meaning ‘full of’ (here پر آواز = ‘full of sound/cries’) and پرخاشجوی is an adjective modifying آواز ‘aggressive/quarrelsome’. So literally ‘the world became full of aggressive cries.’
segments.s648-g177: غو (uproar) stands first as the main noun and is followed by پاسبانان (guards) in an implicit genitive relation: 'the uproar of the guards.' The coordinating و links that whole phrase to بانگ جرس, where بانگ (cry/sound) + جرس (bell) likewise form 'the bell's peal/sound.' This compact noun stacking without ezafe is typical of classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s648-g178: همی‌آمد is the old/poetic imperfective past form meaning a repeated or continuous past action ('kept coming'). از دور is a prepositional phrase 'from afar.' بر here functions as a preposition 'toward/on' and پیش و پس ('before and after' / 'front and back') is a coordinated locative phrase indicating both directions or sides. Word order is flexible and poetic: verb + source + direction.
segments.s648-g179: چنین تا is a classical conjunctive frame 'thus until'; پدید آمد is a compound of پدید (appear) + آمد (came) meaning 'appeared.' از introduces the source/agent 'from the blade' (میغ), and the following شید qualifies that blade (here 'شید' functions as a name/epithet 'the beloved' or 'the radiant one'). Together the phrase means 'until the blade of Shīd/the beloved appeared.'
segments.s648-g180: در و دشت is an idiomatic binary pair meaning built places (houses/gates/towns) and the plain — 'everywhere, town and field.' شد is the past copula 'became.' چون is the comparative 'like,' followed by بلور سپید 'white crystal' as the simile. The whole clause: 'town and plain became like white crystal.'
segments.s648-g181: دو لشکر is the plural subject 'two armies.' همی here is the classical adverbial particle accompanying the action (often giving a continuous or emphatic shade) and جنگ را uses را as the definite object marker, so جنگ را ساختند literally 'they made/raised/laid out the battle' — idiomatically 'they engaged in/prepared for battle.' ساختند is past plural 'they made/prepared.'
segments.s648-g182: درفش بزرگی is a noun phrase where درفش (standard, banner) is qualified by بزرگی — here describing its size or grandeur ('a great banner'). برافراختند is a past plural verb 'they raised/hoisted up.' The verb applies to the preceding noun phrase: 'they raised a great banner.'
segments.s648-g183: از introduces the source 'from' or 'at the sound of.' آواز گردان is a genitival pairing 'the cry/sound of the troop/rank' (گردان = troop/unit). پرخاشخر is an adjectival qualification 'hostile/aggressive,' so the whole phrase means 'from the cry of the hostile ranks.'
segments.s648-g184: بدرید is a past transitive 'tore.' مر is the old clipped form of مرا ('me' / 'to me') functioning here as an object/dative ('my' / 'for me'). The phrase اژدها را جگر places the possessor (اژدها 'the dragon') with the object marker را before جگر (the head noun 'liver'/'heart'), a poetic inversion; together it denotes 'the dragon's heart/liver.' Literally: 'they tore, to me, the dragon's heart' — i.e. 'the dragon's heart was torn (for me).'
segments.s648-g297: یکایک (one by one) is an adverbial/quantifier placed before the verb. فرستید here is an imperative/command form addressed to (plural or respectful singular) 'send/bring'. نزدیک من is a prepositional/locative phrase (literally ‘near me’), with من as the object of proximity. The sequence quantifier + verb + locative is a normal poetic order for giving an instruction.
segments.s648-g298: به پیش is a preposition + noun combination meaning ‘to/in the presence of’ (به = to/for; پیش = in the presence of). بزرگان is the plural noun ‘great ones/elders’ and این انجمن (this assembly) is the specifying noun phrase that follows to identify which assembly; انجمن is the head noun and این points to the current/aforementioned assembly.
segments.s648-g299: به + nouns (به تاراج و کشتن) marks the purpose/area ('for pillage and killing'). نیازیم is first‑person plural present ‘we (are in) need’ (in context read idiomatically as ‘have no need’ or ‘need not’), and دست here functions as the thing wanted/not wanted — ‘a hand’ in the sense of help/force. Word order places the purpose phrase before the verb and its object (دست), a common poetic ordering.
segments.s648-g300: که here is the causal conjunction ‘for/because’. ما is the subject ‘we’. بی‌نیازیم is a predicative adjective with the first‑person plural ending meaning ‘we are without need / self‑sufficient’. و + یزدان‌پرست adds a coordinating predicate ‘and (we are) God‑worshippers/pious’, so the whole clause gives a reason/explanation for the previous statement.
segments.s648-g301: ز جیحون = 'from (or across) the Jeyhun (river)'; ز is the poetic preposition ‘from’. به‌روز here means ‘in a day / by day’ (i.e. within a day). بگذریم is first‑person plural jussive/subjunctive ‘let us pass / let us cross’, a hortatory expression inviting collective action.
segments.s648-g302: وزان پس combines وزان (‘and from that / and then’) + پس (‘after/thereafter’), signaling a subsequent action. پیی is a noun meaning ‘pursuit’/‘following’ (here the sense of continuing attention or effort toward the land). خاک را uses را as the definite object marker (‘the land/soil’). نسپریم is the negative first‑person plural subjunctive/imperative formed on سپردن with negative ن‑: ‘let us not entrust / let us not give up’. Together the clause forbids surrendering the land after the crossing.
segments.s648-g303: همه هرچ 'all/whatever' introduces the material to be heeded. گفتم is the speaker’s past tense ‘I said’ (here referring to what was just said). تو را uses را as an object/emphatic marker addressing 'you'. گوش‌دار is an admonitory predicate meaning ‘be heedful / pay attention’ (here vocative/imperative in sense: 'listen!').
segments.s648-g304: چو رفتی is a temporal conditional clause: چو = 'when/when you' and رفتی = second‑person past ('you go/you have gone' used as ‘when you depart’). یکایک (one by one) modifies the following verb. برو is the imperative 'go/leave' and برشمار is a compound verb here meaning 'count up/enumerate' (imperative/infinitival sense); together: 'when you leave, go and count them one by one.'
segments.s648-g89: Literal order: 'یکی' (a certain/one) + noun + verb + post‑posed adjective. بگزید is the literary past tense 'he chose/selected' (ب‑ as an archaic/preformative element). بیداردل is an adjectival epithet ('wakeful‑hearted') describing the man; placed after the verb in this poetic line rather than immediately after the noun.
segments.s648-g90: که introduces a relative clause modifying 'man' ('who/that'). آهسته is an adverb of manner here = 'softly/gently'. دارد is the present form of داشتن used here to mean 'he has/keeps' (i.e. 'he keeps/holds [things] in a certain way'). به + گفتار دل is a prepositional phrase 'in/with respect to the speech of the heart' (گفتار 'speech', دل 'heart'), so the clause says he keeps the heart's words gentle.
segments.s648-g91: نویسنده is the agent 'the scribe/writer' (here the one speaking). نامه را has the object marker را on 'نامه' ('the letter' as definite object/target of address). گفت is the verb 'said' (here introducing direct speech), and خیز is the imperative 'Rise!' quoted as what he said.
segments.s648-g92: که here gives the reason/explanation ('for/because'). آمد is past 'came' or 'has come'. سر خامه را (lit. 'the head of the quill') is marked with را and functions as the theme affected by what 'has come'. رستخیز ('resurrection'/'uprising') is the substantive denoting a rising; the clause says that the pen's tip has come to a rising — a poetic way to announce a stirring or beginning.
segments.s648-g93: Imperative construction: بنویس is the 'write!' command. یکی restricts نامه to 'a single/one letter'. زی here is an archaic/interjectional vocative form (close to 'ای' in poetry) addressing the addressee; خوشنواز is the vocative epithet 'O melodious/pleasant‑sounding one' or 'skilled musician' addressed by the speaker.
segments.s648-g94: که‌ای functions as the opening of the quoted/direct speech (poetically cliticized 'که' + vocative 'ای'): 'that is, “O...”.' بی‌خرد is the vocative 'O fool' (an insulting address). روبه here is the prepositional/directional sense 'toward' (not the animal 'fox'). دیوساز is an agent noun 'demon‑maker'/'devil‑maker' (one who makes/trains demons or a troublemaker) and is the object of روبه.
segments.s648-g95: گنهکار کردی is a causative/performative phrasing 'you made (yourself) sinful' or more simply 'you sinned / you became a sinner' (کردی = 'you did'). به یزدان marks the moral addressee/standard 'before/to God' (i.e. in God's sight). تنت is 'your body' (تن + second‑person suffix).
segments.s648-g96: شود is used as a resultive/future 'will become' (third person). مویه‌گر is a noun 'mourner/wailer' (one who laments). بر تو پیراهنت is a prepositional phrase with بر 'upon/on' and the possessor sequence 'تو' + پیراهنت ('your tunic/garment'); poetically the line says the body will be a mourner upon your garment — i.e. you will lament over your clothing (a conventional expression of mourning).
segments.s648-g41: برفتند is a past 3rd-person plural verb ('they departed/went away'). خشنود is a predicative/adverbial adjective ('pleased'), describing the manner of their departing. ز + ایوان اوی is a prepositional phrase meaning 'from his iwan/porch': ز is the poetic preposition 'from' and اوی is the enclitic possessive 'his'.
segments.s648-g42: به marks the recipient ('to/into') and introduces the phrase 'entrusted to God'. سپرده here is a past participle/remote perfect ('entrusted') used predicatively; the construction is verbless (copula omitted) so 'تن و جان اوی' ('his body and soul') are the things described as 'entrusted' and اوی is the possessive 'his'.
segments.s648-g43: بدآنگه is a temporal/discourse adverb meaning roughly 'then/after that' (archaic/poetic form of به آن‌گاه); که introduces the subordinate clause ('when/that') and پیروز شد is a 3rd-person past construction where پیروز is an adjective 'victorious' and شد is 'became'. سوی جنگ is the prepositional phrase 'toward the battle' with سوی = 'toward'.
segments.s648-g44: یکی is an indefinite 'one / a certain', followed by پهلوان 'champion/hero' as the subject; جست is the simple past ('leapt/sprang forth') and follows the subject in this older narrative word order. با introduces means/instrument ('with' or 'by'), رای here means 'plan/counsel' (not merely 'opinion'), و links رای and سنگ, and سنگ is the literal 'stone' used as an implement.
segments.s648-g45: که introduces a purpose/optative clause ('so that / in order that'). باشد is the subjunctive/present-form of 'to be' used optatively here ('may be / should be'). نگهبان is the predicate noun 'guardian', and تخت و کلاه lists the objects of guardianship ('throne and [figurative] crown'); word order is subjunctive clause + predicate, with the subject implied (3rd person).
segments.s648-g46: بلاش is the proper name 'Balash'. جوان here characterizes him ('the young man' or 'youth') and را functions here as a topical/emphatic marker attached to that phrase (classical usage). بود is the past copula 'was', and نیکخواه is a predicate noun/adjective meaning 'well-wisher' or 'benevolent one'.
segments.s648-g47: بدان is an older fused form corresponding to به آن ('to/for that'); orthographically here it appears as ب + دان (دان in the list is actually the fossilized element corresponding to 'آن' rather than the verb 'to know'). کار شایسته is the noun phrase 'worthy task/work'. بد immediately before سوفزای attaches to the name as a descriptive element or nickname (literally 'bad' but functioning as part of the personal epithet), and سوفزای is the personal name.
segments.s648-g48: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain / one', نامور is an adjective 'renowned/famed' modifying that one, بود is the past copula 'was', and پاکیزه‌رای is a compound adjective meaning 'pure‑minded' or 'of pure opinion', serving as the predicate describing that famed person.
segments.s648-g113: نمانم is first-person negative ('I will not remain / I will not leave [it]'); جهان را uses the object marker را after 'world' so 'the world' is the thing affected. بفرزند here is a poetic contraction of به فرزند ('to/for the son/offspring') and links the object to the intended beneficiary; تو is the 2nd-person possessive pronoun 'your'. Word order (verb — object — beneficiary) is typical of Persian poetry where the beneficiary phrase follows the object.
segments.s648-g114: نه begins a second negative clause ('nor'); بر is the preposition 'on/upon' (here introducing what the speaker will not leave to the addressee). دوده 'soot/ashes' is a metaphorical term, خوی + ش forms خویش with the enclitic ش 'his/its' (here functioning as a possessive), and پیوند 'bond/alliances' completes the list; تو at the end is the possessor 'your'. The series is coordinated by و 'and'.
segments.s648-g115: بفرمان یزدان is an agent/ablative phrase 'by order of God' placed before the verb for emphasis. ببرم is the first-person verb (classical/persian present/subjunctive form with a future/intent sense) from بردن 'to take/cut off' — here 'I will take' — and سرت is the object with the possessive suffix '‑ت' meaning 'your head'.
segments.s648-g116: ز is the poetic short form of از 'from/because of' and begins a manner/result phrase 'from blood'; همچو دریا is a simile 'like a sea' (همچو = 'like'), کنَم is the 1st-person present/optative of کردن 'to make' (here 'I will make' or 'I will turn'), and کشورت is 'your country/realm' with the 2nd-person suffix. The clause therefore means 'I will make your realm (be) like a sea of blood.'
segments.s648-g117: نه کین باشد is a negated copular clause: نه + کین + باشد = 'this is not vengeance' (باشد used as the copula 'is' / optative 'let it be' depending on context). این چند گویم دراز literally 'I say these few long [words]': این چند ('these few') modifies گویم (here used as 'I say' / 'my words' in poetic usage) and دراز 'long' qualifies them. The clause states the speaker's denial and introduces that he speaks at length.
segments.s648-g118: که introduces an explanatory/reason clause ('for/because'); از کین = 'out of/from vengeance' (از used here in the causal sense); پیروز is an adjective/noun 'the victorious one'; با 'with' links to خوشنواز which can be read as an epithet or a companion 'the melodious/skilled-player'. Grammatically this clause gives the reason or circumstance tied to the preceding remark.
segments.s648-g119: شود is the optative/impersonal 'become' or 'may it be' (expressing a wish), زیر خاک 'under the earth' is a locative phrase ('beneath the ground'), پی من 'after/in pursuit of me' ('in my wake' — پی as 'following'), and تباه 'ruined/perished' is the predicate adjective. Together this is a wish/curse: 'May he be ruined beneath the earth after me.'
segments.s648-g120: به یزدان introduces a dative/to-God formula ('to/with God'); روانش is the soul with the enclitic possessive 'his' (روان + ش), بود is the past copula 'was', and دادخواه is a substantive 'seeker of justice / avenger'. The clause reads as a short wish plus an appraisal: '[May] his soul [be] with God; he was a seeker of justice.'
segments.s648-g81: کنون (adverb) sets the time: “now.” من is the explicit first‑person subject “I.” به + دستوری expresses agency/instrument: here به means “by” and دستوری (literally “commanding/command”) functions as a noun meaning “(the) command.” شهریار is the noun modified by دستوری: “the king’s command.” Word order is topical (time + subject + prepositional phrase).
segments.s648-g82: بسیجم is a first‑person form meaning “I muster / I will muster” (present/optative feel in classical Persian; here a declarative commitment). برین گونه is a unit: بر + ین (orthographic variant of این) + گونه = “in this manner/way” (بر used as part of the demonstrative phrase, گونه = manner). The second بر before کارزار is a preposition “to/toward” (بر کارزار = “to the battle / into battle”).
segments.s648-g83: کزین is a fused prepositional phrase (کز + این) meaning “from/ because of this,” introducing the cause. کینه و خون are coordinated nouns “hatred and blood(bloodshed).” پیروز precedes and qualifies شاه as an epithet: “the victorious king.” Adjective before noun is common in this style.
segments.s648-g84: بنالد is a third‑person singular present/imperfect verb meaning “he laments /moans.” The particle ز introduces the source/reason (“from/because of”), followed by the compound noun phrase چرخ روان (چرخ = sky/heaven or cosmic wheel, modified by روان 'moving/flowing') and then the coordinated subject هور و ماه (“sun and moon”); the verb appears before some of its modifiers here because of poetic inversion.
segments.s648-g85: فرستاده is the subject “the messenger.” زین = “saddle” and روی here functions as the locative/prepositional element “on/upon” (so زین روی + برداشت پای = ‘he lifted/removed his foot from the saddle’). برداشت is a past/prefixed perfect meaning “he lifted/removed,” and پای is the direct object “foot.” The verb + object order reflects classical usage and a slight inversion for poetic diction.
segments.s648-g86: وزان is a connective “and/from that” (linking this action to what follows). سوی governs direction “toward,” and گریان is a predicative adjective/participle “weeping” (describing his manner). بشد is the past/become verb “he became / he turned” (here “he became weeping” = “he went/was weeping”), and باز جای = “back to his place” (باز “back/again” + جای “place”), i.e. he returned to his post. Word order keeps the vivid, compressed narrative style.
segments.s648-g87: بیاراست is a past/transitive verb “(he) arrayed / arranged / adorned” (here applied to troops). لشکر is the object “army/host.” چو introduces a simile “as/like.” پر is poetic here (literal “feathers” or “full of”) used in the simile, and تذرو (an action/poetic noun) conveys dispersion/ fluttering—together producing the image “like scattered/ fluttering feathers.” The line uses a simile to describe the army’s appearance or movement.
segments.s648-g88: بیامد is simple past third singular “(it) came.” ز is the poetic preposition “from,” followed by the place name زاولستان (Zaulistan). سوی governs direction “toward,” and مرو is the destination “Merv.” Sequence: the army was arrayed, and then it came from Zaulistan toward Merv.
segments.s648-g97: به شاه آنک تو کردی‌ای بیوفا is a clause headed by the prepositional phrase به شاه ('to/with respect to the king') followed by the relative/anaphoric آنک ('that/whom') linking to what follows. تو کردی is the 2nd-person perfective 'you did', here addressed with the vocative particle ای attached (کردی‌ای) and completed by the epithet بیوفا ('O unfaithful one'). This line omits an explicit verb for the whole larger construction, so this segment establishes the referent ('to the king whom you treated, O faithless one').
segments.s648-g98: ببینی is a present/future-form verb with the prefixed ب- ('you will/shall see' or 'may you see'); کنون is the temporal adverb 'now'. زور تیغ جفا is a noun-chain where زور ('strength, might') is modified by تیغ جفا ('the sword of cruelty/injustice') — a possessive/genitive-like apposition common in Persian poetry. So the verb takes this noun phrase as its object: 'now you will see the might of the sword of cruelty.'
segments.s648-g99: به کشتی here uses به with a locative/motion sense ('into/on/onto the ship'). شهنشاه را uses the definite marker را after 'Shahanshah' — in classical/poetic syntax this particle can appear before the head noun or in constructions linking possessor and possessed; together with بی‌گناه ('innocent') it signals 'the innocent (one) of the Shahanshah'. The verb ('they put' or an equivalent copula) is omitted from the line and understood from the narrative.
segments.s648-g100: نبیره is the noun 'scion/son/heir' and is followed by apposition of titles جهاندار بهرام شاه — 'the world-holder (i.e. sovereign) Bahram, king.' Persian poetic word order places these epithets and the proper name after the noun; no copula is needed. The phrase identifies the person placed/held on the ship.
segments.s648-g101: یکی functions here as an indefinite 'a/certain'; کین نو is the noun + adjective 'a new hatred/grudge' (adjective follows noun). ساختی is 2nd-person perfective 'you made/created'. در جهان is a locative phrase 'in the world' placed after the verb as is common in Persian. Altogether: 'you made/created a new grudge in the world.'
segments.s648-g102: که is the relative/conjunctive 'that' linking to the previous clause. آن کینه 'that grudge' (demonstrative + noun) is the subject of the predication هرگز نگردد نهان: هرگز 'never' + نگردد (from گرد/گشت with negative prefix ن-) 'become/turn' in a subjunctive/negative present-future sense, and نهان 'hidden' is the predicate adjective. Poetic word order places the predicate after the verb: 'that that grudge will never become hidden.'
segments.s648-g103: چرا is the interrogative 'why'. پیش او means 'in his presence / before him' (پیش + pronoun او). چون is here 'like/as', and یکی is an indefinite 'a' (one) introducing the simile 'یکی چابلوس' — 'a sycophant'. The phrase asks why the addressee did not act obsequiously in the presence of that person.
segments.s648-g104: نرفتی is the negative past 2nd-person 'you did not go' (ن- negative + رفتی). چو here functions temporally as 'when', introducing the subordinate clause چو برخاست آوای کوس: برخاست is past 'rose/arose' and آوای کوس 'the sound/call of the war-drum' (آوا + کوس). The whole clause reads 'you did not go when the call of the drum was raised.'
segments.s648-g345: This clause has the subject (بلاش) first, a time adverbial (آن زمان) immediately after it, then the past-tense verb (دید). The direct object is the noun phrase روی قباد (literally “the face of Qobad”), with روی (face) following the verb as is common in Persian word order here; دید is simple past, 3rd person singular "saw/ beheld."
segments.s648-g346: رها گشته is a compound participial/predicate construction: رها (free/unbound) + گشته (perfect participle/"become/been") — together meaning "having been freed" or "was freed." از بند is the prepositional phrase "from the fetter(s)/bond(s)," and پیروز و شاد are post‑positional adjectives (conjoined) describing the resulting state "victorious and joyful."
segments.s648-g347: مر here is an archaic dative/oblique pronoun form rendered "me/to me" in the lemma list but in this construction functions as a short pronominal particle tied to the scene; او را is the direct object marker phrase "him" (او + object marker را), سبک qualifies the manner "light/gentle," and شاه is the subject; در برگرفت is a compound verb (در + برگرفت) meaning "took up/embraced" (past tense, 3rd sing.). The clause therefore means the king gently took him up/embraced him.
segments.s648-g348: ز is the poetic preposition "from." هیتال و چین are proper names (here acting as the source from which the king takes the person), دست بر سر گرفت is a verbal phrase literally "took a hand upon the head" — idiomatically describing placing a hand on someone's head (a gesture of favour/blessing) or taking him away from those people and setting a hand on his head. گرفت is past tense "took/seized."
segments.s648-g349: ز راه is the prepositional phrase "from the road." اندر ایوان شاه is a locative phrase "into/within the king's iwan/hall" (اندر = in/within, ایوان = iwan/porch), and آمدند is the past plural verb "they came." Word order places the source and location before the verb, as typical in classical Persian narrative.
segments.s648-g350: Two predicative adjectives (گشاده‌دل and نیک‌خواه) appear before the verb آمدند; they describe the manner/attitude of the arrivals: "open‑hearted/generous and benevolent/well‑wishing." The coordinating و links the two adjectives, and آمدند (they came) closes the clause in past plural.
segments.s648-g351: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past-tense verb meaning "he ordered/commanded/said (politely)." تا here introduces a purpose/result clause "so that / that," خوان is the banquet/table, and بیاراستند is past plural "they arranged/set/arrayed." Together: "He commanded that they set the table/prepare the feast."
segments.s648-g352: می‌و و رود is a paratactic pairing meaning "wine and (musical) stream/instrument" — رود here is used in the sense of a musical instrument or musical accompaniment (not a literal river). رامشگران خواستند: خواستند is past plural "they desired/called for/asked," and رامشگران are those who bring comfort/entertainment (here "entertainers/musicians/comforters"). The clause therefore reports that they requested wine, music, and performers.
